claim: Al20Zn80 at 870K is a solid at equilibrium
Al and Zn form a eutectic around 88 at % Zn
Al50Zn50 forms a single solid phase <700K
The phase fraction of Aluminum Silicon Carbide precipitates in Al30Si55C15 increases with decreasing temperature from 500K to 300K
Heating a sample of Al30Si55C15 to 1500K is insufficient to dissolve all carbide precipitates
The equilibrium open-circuit potential for a lithium battery with an AlMg anode is ~0.5 V vs. Li/Li+
Alloying Mg into an Al anode increases the voltage of a lithium battery.
Cu80Li10Al10 can form a thermodynamically stable anode for a lithium battery
For a (CuAl)$_{1-x}$Li$_{x}$ electrode, the maximum practical capacity is x=0.4
The initial reaction of Li with AlCu is a two phase reaction
The bulk modulus of Silver is ~100GPa
Doping Ag with 12.5% Cu increases the bulk modulus by 20%.
At low temperatures there are no distortions in the VO6 octahedra VO2
Gold diffusion barrier on aluminum surfaces is < 0.2eV
The barrier for Li hopping between sites in a graphite electrode is <0.1eV
Increasing spacing along the c-axis stabilizes the Cu-O octahedral in La2CuO4.
Someone made a stronger magnet by doping \alpha iron oxide (Fe2O3 trigonal) with aluminum
Phosphorus interstitials in Si are unstable compared to substitutional doping
At STP, nitrogen doping of GaAs at Ga sites is more stable than at As sites.
A mixture of 30 at.% Fe and 70 at.% Al at a temperature of 750 K results thermodynamically in a two-phase solid mixture of Al13Fe4 and Al-rich BCC Fe.
A mixture of 30 at.% Fe and 70 at.% Al at a temperature of 750 K results thermodynamically in a two-phase solid mixture of Al5Fe2 and Al2Fe.
A mixture of 30 at.% Fe and 70 at.% Al at a temperature of 750 K results thermodynamically in a two-phase solid mixture that consists of 5% phase fraction Al5Fe2 and 95% phase fraction Al2Fe.
In binary iron-aluminum ferroalloys, increasing the mole fraction of aluminum beyond about 50 at.% leads to the formation of a secondary Al2Fe phase within the primary iron-based alloy.

In binary iron-aluminum ferroalloys, concentrations of aluminum increasing from about 50 at.% to about 52 at.% lead to small amounts of a secondary intermetallic Al2Fe phase which increases mechanical strength without causing embrittlement.

In binary iron-aluminum ferroalloys, concentrations of aluminum of about 65 at.% lead to small amounts of a secondary intermetallic Al2Fe phase which increases mechanical strength of the primary BCC iron phase without causing embrittlement.

In lightweight alloys consisting mostly of aluminum and small fractions of magnesium (<8 at.%) and zinc (<4 at.%), slowly solidifying from the melt leads to a mechanically desirable two-phase equilibrium between a primary fcc phase and up to 20% fraction of a three-component "tau" phase.

In aluminum-based lightweight alloys including 8 at.% magnesium and 4 at.% zinc, slowly solidifying from the melt leads to a mechanically desirable equilibrium mixture between a primary fcc phase and two secondary phases: a Laves-type MgZn phase and a gamma AlMg phase.

In the Al-Mg-Zn system, adding just a few atomic percent Zn to the eutectic Al-Mg composition with about 34.5 at.% Mg leads to a significant amount of the three-component "tau" phase: over 20% phase fraction in the resulting alloy.
The saturation magnetization of hematite iron oxide (Fe2O3) can be increased by up to 10% by doping cobalt onto the iron sites.

Several variations of magnetic metal oxides were tested based on the host hematite Fe2O3 material, and among Ni, Co, and Nd dopants, cobalt produced the least degradation in the saturation magnetization.
The saturation magnetization of Co3O4 cobalt oxide can be significantly increased by doping iron (Fe) onto the cobalt sites.
The saturation magnetization of Co3O4 cobalt oxide can be significantly increased by doping neodymium (Nd) onto the cobalt sites.

Copper is useful as an additive in lightweight aluminum alloys. Depending on the concentration, some of it may precipitate out into secondary phases. Strictly within the primary aluminum phase, the portion of copper that is soluble has the effect of significantly increasing the elastic modulus or stiffness of the primary phase.

Copper is useful as an additive in lightweight aluminum alloys. Depending on the concentration, some of it may precipitate out into secondary phases. Strictly within the primary aluminum phase, the portion of copper that is soluble has the effect of significantly decreasing the elastic modulus or stiffness of the primary phase.

Magnesium is useful as an additive in lightweight aluminum alloys. Depending on the concentration, some of it may precipitate out into secondary phases. Strictly within the primary aluminum phase, the portion of magnesium that is soluble has the effect of significantly increasing the elastic modulus or stiffness of the primary phase.

Among Cu, Mg, and Zn, which are all common additives in aluminum alloys, magnesium substitutional defects cause the largest increase in the equilibrium lattice parameter of the primary aluminum fcc phase. This is relevant for knowing the relative solubility of the different additives as well as intrinsic lattice strain that may result from alloying and affecting resulting mechanical properties.

As an additive in aluminum alloys, copper substitutional defects cause a moderate increase in the equilibrium lattice parameter of the primary aluminum fcc phase. This is relevant for anticipating the relative solubility compared to other additives as well as intrinsic lattice strain that may result from alloying and affecting resulting mechanical properties.
